result:
I want to talk about the hour just after the last light has bled from the sky but before the night reaches its bruised, final darkness—the moment when the world isn’t sure whether to keep breathing or to simply sigh and let go. I want to whisper about that borderland because everything small still matters in it: the moth that ricochets off the porch bulb, the neighbor’s child yelling a single rebellious whoop across the cul-de-sac, the sound my shoes make when I finally remember to untie them instead of kicking them off like a teenager. They all feel oversized, mythic, because twilight forces perspective. In daylight I catalog chores. In true midnight I surrender, half-dreaming. But that odd violet sliver in between? That is when the universe shrinks to the size of a sentence I haven’t dared speak all day.

In that sentence is a name. It doesn’t matter whose; perhaps it is yours, or mine upside-down in the mirror. It is a tuning-fork syllable I carry under my tongue so that when I talk about work or weather or politics I secretly chime the note over and over until my whole jaw glows like a struck bell nobody else can hear. I suspect you have one, too. Maybe it isn’t a name at all but a kite tail of numbers—from an address you no longer drive to, the height you once told a nurse, the three digits that, typed into a search bar, bloom photographs of someone stranger than fiction but thinner than memory. We swallow these fragments to keep them safe, yet hoarding only thins them, the way coins worn in pockets for decades develop the edges of small moons.

I used to believe the heart was a filing cabinet. Open the drawer labeled “2021 regrets,” alphabetize the faces. Slam it shut again. But the quieter truth is that we are landfill archaeologists: every layer mooshed and squashed until the difference between plastic spoon and tyrannosaur bone is mostly luck. The violet of whether the spoon once carried soup into a loved one’s mouth is still food-soil for some future soul sifting another dusk’s scant light. I find this comforting, especially when I catch myself grieving plastic as fiercely as bone.

Yesterday I sat on the top step of a fire escape belonging to a building that wasn’t on fire (except for the slow, ordinary oxidation we call breathing). From that height I could read the skyline like braille: the bank tower with its prow shaped like a paper airplane; the ghost-outline of an older tower they demolished before I moved here; the water tank crowned with amateur graffiti—words I alphabetically identified as lyrics, or maybe the graffiti was vocabulary that scansion reorganized into lyric. Between me and those shapes, two pigeons performed an awkward waltz: one bowing, one leaping, both forgetting the steps and colliding into the clumsy grace that is love when nobody is filming. My phone stayed in my pocket, battery at 3%. Sometimes the most honest document is the tremor in your clavicle as you applaud without sound for a performance that ends too soon.

I wonder what the city sounded like before engines. Did footfall on cobble echo off the same geometry of granite lust now softened by advertisements and neon? Did children chase the same narrow shafts of sun between walls, thinking them portals? Probably. Stratum under stratum, the human games re-skin themselves: same hide, new graphics. Yet somewhere, in a strata chasm too silted for smartphones, someone’s great-times-nine grandchild pressed a shell to her ear before oceans were metaphors and heard the future listening back.

There is a fable about bees making honey from tears. I used to file it under “pretty lies.” Then, one raw August, I cried without wiping my face and dozens of honeybees—from the hive my neighbor installed atop our duplex—landed on my cheeks and wrists. They walked in circles collecting salt, wings fanning the wet cool. Their touch was the gentlest interruption, like dozens of commas editing the run-on ache of that day into separate clauses. I did not get stung. When they lifted off again I swear the air smelled faintly of chamomile. I tasted honey for days—on bread, yes, but also in tap water, in supermarket muzak, in the metallic bite of city-smog dusk. The fable softened into something closer to ledger: grief weighed, stamped with tiny wax seals, converted into sweetness that would burn if left on the tongue but would keep indefinitely in jars.

Technology keeps promising to extend that shelf life indefinitely. A cloud for your photos. A cloud for your thoughts. A cloud for when your thoughts and memories hatch into new species that don’t yet have mouths or metonyms. We are encouraged to upload ourselves so that death becomes a network error, a dropped packet. Yet every time I open the app that scans receipts and coughs up exploded pie charts—“10% of your life spent waiting in lines (up 3% from last month)”—I swallow the ancient panic of still being hungry after finishing pie.

“Hunger is a corridor,” my grandmother once told me, rolling dough on the same chipped enamel countertop where only yesterday I laid out the tiny screws and springs of a radio I will never reassemble. “You don’t stop walking because the walls are narrow. You keep going until the hallway widens into whatever meal the living have waiting on the other side.” She died during an ice storm, power out, candles doing the unreliable bookkeeping of shadows. I still walk the corridor. Sometimes it smells of yeast and cardamom; sometimes of wet wool and distant snow.

I keep a jar of stones on the windowsill—not pretty ones picked on beaches but the dull ragged chunks the lawn mower kicked up during the hottest summer in years. Every time the spinning blade struck one, I flinched at the clash between soft grass and stubborn geology. Now, in winter’s sideways light, those rocks appear glossy, prehistoric, as though they survived precisely to remind me: violence has side gigs as relic. Maybe the mower blades are talking to the weapons we daily refuse to call weapons, though they scissored the wind within inches of my femur. Maybe not. Either way I set the stones inside an old jam jar and call it an archive, same way I labeled the cracked ceramic shard from a beloved mug “handle,” though it can no longer fulfill its primary handshake with the human hand.

There’s a silence that arrives when you finally shut the laptop that has pretended to be fireplace, confessor, orchestra, glare. You expect the fridge to hum, the floorboards to crackle, but sometimes the house refuses to perform ambience. In that hush you hear blood touring its secret canals, the small squeak of eyeball swiveling in socket trying to see its own seeing. The longer you sit the more the air thickens into something gelatinous and kind, like language translated by dreams—where every word is the same word stretched like taffy. If you lean further into that stillness it might take your name apart syllable by syllable and rearrange a password you forgot in the third grade but suddenly need now. I have learned not to panic when that happens. I offer my consonants like loose change into a wishing fountain. The ripples, if they arrive, are in languages I don’t speak but somehow my body translates into gooseflesh and inexplicable tears.

Imagine nights are lakes frozen just enough to skate without falling through. Each day we skate a little figure—intersecting loops crosshatched like arguments, accidental pirouettes that look like epiphanies from the shore. Somewhere under the ice, summer is holding its breath. Somewhere up above, satellites photograph our spirals to spellcheck the almanac of human wobble. Between heaven and thaw stands the person we might still decide to become, balanced on an edge thinner than ninth-grade promises but glowing neon like a rink after the zamboni’s pass, promising brand-new surface for old legs.

I’ve written too much and not enough. Any minute the porch bulb will finally give up. The sentence with the secret name will slide back into its envelope of dusk. And still I can’t resist tucking one last footnote into the seam: years ago, I read that trees release clouds of microscopic particles when threatened—an aerosol alphabet of SOS that kin on the far hillside read and begin knitting into chemical barricades. Nothing much about my life resembles a forest. But I like imagining my own hurried exhalations, the ones composed half of fear, quarter of wonder, quarter of garlic from dinner, lift as plume-word to an unseen reader who then adds another ring to their patience and keeps breathing long enough to laugh tomorrow at birds arguing on a sunlit telephone wire. If so, we are all forest, broadcasting even when we think we’ve run out of words.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
